does: This paper introduces GazeReward, a novel framework that augments the Reward Model component of RLHF by incorporating eye-tracking (ET) data as implicit feedback signals. The authors integrate ET-based features into reward models and evaluate their approach across multiple dimensions: different integration techniques, various LLMs (GPT, Llama, Claude, Gemini), and different ET generator models. Through comprehensive ablation studies on established human preference datasets, they demonstrate that eye-tracking data significantly improves reward model accuracy, showing that gaze patterns provide measurable signals about human preference that traditional explicit feedback misses.
connects: This work builds on the established pipeline of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), which has become standard for aligning LLMs with human values but faces well-documented challenges in accurately capturing human preferences from explicit ratings alone. The paper extends prior research in human-AI alignment by incorporating implicit behavioral signals—specifically borrowing insights from cognitive science and eye-tracking literature that show gaze patterns correlate with attention, preference, and cognitive load. It connects to growing work exploring multimodal and physiological signals for understanding human feedback, moving beyond discrete preference labels toward richer data modalities that capture the nuances of human evaluation.
enables: By demonstrating that eye-tracking data improves reward modeling, this work opens pathways for incorporating other implicit behavioral and physiological signals (facial expressions, attention patterns, response latency) into the alignment process. It suggests future research into cognitive data as a systematic lever for training more human-aligned models, potentially reducing dependence on costly explicit preference annotation. The framework could enable more efficient and nuanced alignment systems that capture genuine human preferences rather than proxy metrics, advancing toward AI systems that better reflect authentic human values. This also motivates development of alignment methods that work with varied data modalities and cognitive signals available in real-world deployment scenarios.
